questions.question: Explain adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations of a graph with a suitable example. Compare their space and time complexities.
keyPoints: Adjacency matrix: V x V array, adj[i][j] = 1 if edge exists
Adjacency list: array of linked lists, one per vertex
Matrix space: O(V^2), List space: O(V+E)
Matrix edge check: O(1), List edge check: O(degree)
Matrix neighbors: O(V), List neighbors: O(degree)
Example graph with both representations drawn

questions.question: For the given graph, construct both the adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations.
keyPoints: Label vertices with indices 0 to n-1
Fill matrix row by row checking edge existence
Verify symmetry for undirected graphs
Build linked list for each vertex with its neighbors
Cross-verify: row sum in matrix = list length for each vertex

questions.question: Compare adjacency matrix and adjacency list representations. When would you prefer one over the other?
keyPoints: Dense graph: matrix preferred (E close to V^2)
Sparse graph: list preferred (E much less than V^2)
Frequent edge queries: matrix gives O(1)
Traversal algorithms (BFS/DFS): list gives O(V+E)
Memory constraint: list is more efficient for sparse graphs

questions.question: Write a C function to convert an adjacency matrix to an adjacency list representation.
keyPoints: Allocate array of V list head pointers
For each row i, scan all columns j
If adj[i][j] = 1, insert j into list[i]
Handle both directed and undirected cases
Time complexity of conversion: O(V^2)

questions.question: Explain how a weighted directed graph is represented using adjacency matrix and adjacency list. Illustrate with an example.
keyPoints: Matrix: adj[i][j] = weight if edge exists, 0 or infinity otherwise
List: each node stores (neighbor, weight) pair
Directed: matrix may be asymmetric
Example with at least 4 vertices and weighted edges
